coredata.dc:title: Mechanical and thermo-oxidative resistance properties of natural rubber film reinforced by orange peel-based carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jing
Liao, Lusheng
Shang, Lingyu
Du, Lijuan
Lai, Yinyi
Liu, Jiahui
Yang, Zinuo
Ma, Yukun
Peng, WenFeng
Zhang, Fuquan
coredata.dc:description: Bio-based carbon dots (CDs) have a wide range of precursor sources, abundant chemical functional groups, and good free radical scavenging ability. This makes them an excellent candidate for modifying natural rubber (NR). In this work, CDs were synthesized from orange peels using a hydrothermal process and were utilized to reinforce NR latex. The structure and properties of both the CDs (including morphology, functional groups and fluorescence emission spectra) and NR/CDs films (including crosslinking density, morphology, functional groups, and mechanical properties) were comprehensively investigated. The results indicate that the inclusion of CDs led to improvements in tensile and tear strength while maintaining undamaged elongation at break. The tube model results indicate that the Ge (the elastic modulus brought by entangled chains) is significantly higher than the Gc (the elastic modulus contributed by chemical crosslinking) in this system, suggesting that the physical entangled network play a more significant role than chemical cross-linking; and the trend of Gc+Ge with the amount of CDs aligns completely with the cross-linking density measured by swelling method. Additionally, it was observed that CDs exhibited excellent thermo-oxidative resistance properties for NR. After 24 hours of thermal aging, the aging coefficient increased from 20.7% (NR-0) to 65.8% (NR-7). Furthermore, with an increase in thermal aging time, the tensile strength and crosslinking density of NR-0 decreased at a faster rate compared with NR-5. At an aging time of 40 hours, the tensile strength of NR-0 was reduced to 0.53 MPa and almost completely failed; whereas the tensile strength of NR-5 was still maintained at 8.2 MPa. Overall, the addition of CDs dramatically improved the thermo-oxidative resistance and mechanical properties of the NR film. Simultaneously, a novel way involving the utilization of orange peel with high value is being proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural rubber latex
Orange peel-based carbon dots
Mechanical properties
Thermo-oxidative resistance performance

China (2019B121203004). Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund (ZDYF2024XDNY284); College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training (X202410579003).

The work were financially supported by Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System, Grant/Award Number: CARS-33-JG2; Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund Grant/Award Number 1630122022006; College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training (X202410579003).

head.ce:title: Mechanical and thermo-oxidative resistance properties of natural rubber film reinforced by orange peel-based carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Lingnan Normal University, Zhanjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Lingnan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Lingnan Normal University; Zhanjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Natural Rubber Processing, Agricultural Products Processing Research Institute of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Zhanjiang 524001, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Natural Rubber Processing, Agricultural Products Processing Research Institute of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tropical Crop Products Processing of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Agricultural Products Processing Research Institute of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Zhanjiang 524001, Guangdong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bio-based carbon dots (CDs) have a wide range of precursor sources, abundant chemical functional groups, and good free radical scavenging ability. This makes them an excellent candidate for modifying natural rubber (NR). In this work, CDs were synthesized from orange peels using a hydrothermal process and were utilized to reinforce NR latex. The structure and properties of both the CDs (including morphology, functional groups and fluorescence emission spectra) and NR/CDs films (including crosslinking density, morphology, functional groups, and mechanical properties) were comprehensively investigated. The results indicate that the inclusion of CDs led to improvements in tensile and tear strength while maintaining undamaged elongation at break. The tube model results indicate that the Ge (the elastic modulus brought by entangled chains) is significantly higher than the Gc (the elastic modulus contributed by chemical crosslinking) in this system, suggesting that the physical entangled network play a more significant role than chemical cross-linking; and the trend of Gc+Ge with the amount of CDs aligns completely with the cross-linking density measured by swelling method. Additionally, it was observed that CDs exhibited excellent thermo-oxidative resistance properties for NR. After 24 hours of thermal aging, the aging coefficient increased from 20.7% (NR-0) to 65.8% (NR-7). Furthermore, with an increase in thermal aging time, the tensile strength and crosslinking density of NR-0 decreased at a faster rate compared with NR-5. At an aging time of 40 hours, the tensile strength of NR-0 was reduced to 0.53 MPa and almost completely failed; whereas the tensile strength of NR-5 was still maintained at 8.2 MPa. Overall, the addition of CDs dramatically improved the thermo-oxidative resistance and mechanical properties of the NR film. Simultaneously, a novel way involving the utilization of orange peel with high value is being proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural rubber (NR) latex is a naturally occurring renewable material that has attracted significant attention due to its exceptional high tensile strength, ease of molding, and minimal creep. NR latex products such as gloves, condoms, transfusion tubes, and catheters are widely utilized in the medical and health industries for their superior performance and durability (Alireza et al.,2024; Chen et al.,2024).

In the family of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a significant member of new nanomaterials. CDs possess several remarkable properties such as exceptional light stability, minimal cytotoxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, easy surface modification and chemical inertness. A wide array of fields has been focused on including cell imaging, fluorescence sensing, photocatalysis, fabrication of multi-color light-emitting diodes (LEDs), energy conversion, bioimaging and drug delivery (Jiang et al., 2024; Santos et al.,2024; Liu et al., 2024; Bartkowski et al., 2024; Kaur and Verma,2022; He et al., 2023). The preparing process of CDs is primarily categorized into two methods of “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The “bottom-up” method involves polymerizing small carbon molecules such as citric acid and glucose into nanoscale carbon particles through hydrothermal, pyrolysis, microwave radiation, ultrasound processes (Zhu et al., 2015). This method offers the advantages of simple reaction conditions, convenient implementation, an abundant carbon source, and low cost. Therefore, it is currently utilized for the preparation of the majority of CDs.

In recent years, biomass-based CDs have been prepared from natural plants and their derivatives (Dong et al., 2024).Biomass contains a significant amount of carbon elements and abundant heteroatoms, making it a cost-effective and low-toxicity resource. The green precursor material played a crucial role in enabling the large-scale production of CDs (on a scale of a few grams to over a hundred grams) (Du et al.,2021). The research of preparation of CDs from different agricultural wastes such as sugarcane bagasse, pineapple, rice husks, tobacco stems, rubber seed shells, coconut shells, corn, banana peel, Ginkgo biloba leaves, lignin is emerging (Jagannathan et al., 2021; 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). The preparation process generally includes collecting raw materials, washing, drying, mechanical grinding, and activation treatment.

It has been demonstrated that CDs possess the ability to scavenge free radicals and are anticipated to become a customizable and effective thermo-oxidative resistance in the fields of biomedical and polymers. Wu et al. synthetized CDs through a simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid, then passivated CDs with ethylenediamine (EDA), urea, 1,4-butanediamine, and triethylamine, respectively, The passivated CDs were then used as thermo-oxidative resistances for styrene butadiene rubber(SBR). In comparison to traditional thermo-oxidative resistance 4010NA, EDA passivated CDs exhibited the strongest photoluminescence intensity and a more potent antioxidant effect on SBR (Wu et al., 2015). Yu et al. employed CDs as reinforcing agents and antioxidants for SBR, with results indicating that well-dispersed CDs can enhance the mechanical for rubber. Furthermore, CDs also demonstrate good antioxidant properties of rubber (Yu et al., 2020). Huangfu et al. has shown that CDs can be directly used as an effective antioxidant for NR without any purification. The findings revealed that after 6 days of aging, the relative strength of rubber samples (A(CO)/A(CH2)) was in the order of NR>NR/ N-1,3-dimethylbutyl-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (4020) >NR/CDs, indicating that both antioxidant 4020 and CDs can delay the thermal oxidation of NR; however, CDs exhibits higher antioxidant efficiency than 4020 (Huangfu et al., 2021).

The global annual production of citrus fruits exceeds 160 million tons, making it the top-ranked fruit in terms of production. As a result, a significant amount of peel is produced annually (Espinosa et al., 2022). Citrus peel is commonly utilized in the production of animal feed, organic fertilizers, biofuels, reinforcing filler for polymer (Ankita and Kuldeep, 2019; Naik et al., 2023; Alshahrani and Prakash, 2024; Prabhu et al., 2024), biosorbent materials for heavy metals and dyes (Deshmukh et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2019; Rahman et al., 2023;Ozturk et al., 2018; Salem et al., 2022). and as natural antioxidants (Yu et al., 2022; Can-Cauich et al., 2017).

As previously mentioned, there is significant potential and attractive benefits in using CDs and orange peels as natural antioxidants and reinforcing fillers. This is especially evident in the preparation of CDs from biomass, which offers advantages such as a wide source of raw materials, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Despite these advantages, there is limited in-depth research on the utilization in NR. In this study, CDs were synthesized using orange peel and glycerol as raw materials. Subsequently, the unpurified CDs were used as a modifier for NR. In this work, the dual antioxidant and reinforcing effects of orange peel based CDs on NR was investigated. The properties of interest included the morphology (TEM) and active functional groups (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy; FTIR) of CDs. The structure and properties of NR films with varying amounts of orange peel-based CDs (ranging from 0 to 7 phr, refers to the quantity of orange peel utilized in the preparation of CDs.) were investigated. This included crosslinking density, mechanical properties, morphology (SEM), functional group analysis, and thermo-oxidative resistance performance. Furthermore, the mechanical properties, structure, and crosslinking density of NR composites with different aging times have been studied.

Concentrated NR latex (60%) was supplied by Hainan Natural Rubber Industry Group Co., Ltd. (Hainan, China). The fatty alcohol polyoxyethylene ether (levelling Agent O-25), zinc oxide (ZnO), Zinc diethyl dithiocarbamate (ZDC), sulfur (S), N-cyclohexyl-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine（antioxidant 4010）, cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and toluene were purchased from the market. The orange peel (navel orange) was collected from the local market in Zhanjiang, Guangdong, China.

After the orange peel was cleaned, it was placed in an drying oven (80℃) for complete drying. Subsequently a crushing machine (JJ-666, Miadkang) was employed to form powder, and large particles were removed via a 60-mesh sieve. The orange peel powder (OPP) was then mixed with glycerol and distilled water at a weight ratio of 1:2:15, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 30 minutes. Subsequently, it was placed in a Teflon-lined 100 mL stainless steel reactor hydrothermal reactor and subjected to a reaction at 160℃ for 6 hours. Once cooled down, the suspension containing CDs was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The pH was adjusted to 8–9 with 2 mol/L KOH. Subsequently, the total mass of the CDs suspension was weighted. Then, the concentration of CDs was converted to the mass of OPP(OPP(g)/CDs suspension (mL)). The subsequent experiments will calculate the amount of CDs based on this conversion. The resulting CDs suspension weighing 2.0 g underwent concentration by heating at 80℃ in an oven before being freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder for testing.

NR latex was filtered using a 400-mesh steel mesh. An amount of CTAB (5%) and levelling agent O-25 solution (10%) were added to the NR latex. Additionally, a certain amount of CDs suspension and compounds were introduced according to the formula shown in Table 1. Subsequently, an appropriate amount of distilled water was added to adjust the solid content of NR mixture to approximately 40%. The mixture was stirred at 200 rpm for 1.0 hour before being sealed with cling film and subjected to stirring at 80 rpm for 2.0 hours in a water bath at 46℃ to pre-vulcanize. It was then rapidly cooled to room temperature and left to mature for 24 hours. Hydrophilic CDs can be effectively dispersed within water-based NR latex; however, the interaction between non-polar NR and polar CDs remains relatively weak. Consequently, CTAB is introduced as a compatibilizer between NR and CDs.

The matured NR mixture underwent filtration with a 400-mesh steel mesh to remove larger particles before being evenly distributed into three levelled clean glass plate molds (26 ×13 cm). The NR composite was allowed time to solidify and take shape within the molds. Once solidified, the NR films were placed in drying oven at 50℃ for 3–5 hours until they could be easily peeled off from the glass plate molds. After soaking the films in water for 2 h, the next step involved drying them in a drying oven at 78℃ for 3 h.The name given to each NR film depended on the quantity of CDs suspension used; thus named as:NR-x (x representing the amount of orange peels used to prepare CDs). A Schematic illustration depicting the experimental process of CDs and NR films is presented in Scheme 1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was conducted using a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on an XL-30 (Philips, Japan), with the samples being coated with a thin layer of gold prior to observation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA), scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 under attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The FTIR measurements for CDs were carried out after mixing them with KBr. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths were measured using a Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh instruments, UK).

The tensile and tear properties of NR composites were tested according to ASTM D412–16ε1 and ASTM D624 by a material tester (3365, Instron Co. USA). Each sample was tested five times in parallel, and the mechanical properties were reported based on the median value. The representative tensile curves were selected from the strain-stress curve corresponding to this median 500% modulus.

The equilibrium swelling method was utilized to determine the crosslinking density of the NR composites. Approximately 0.2 g (m1) of NR film was placed into sample bottles, to which toluene was added for immersion, and then left at room temperature for 72 h. Subsequently, the film was removed from the bottle, its surface immediately wiped, and then placed into a weighing bottle. The mass of the swollen NR film (m2) was accurately measured, three times in parallel of each sample.The crosslinking density (
V
e
) of NR composites was calculated using the following formula and related parameters (Arroyo et al., 2003; Chen et al., 2023).
(1)
V

W

Vr is the volume fraction of the swollen rubber phase, W
s (m2-m1) and W
d (αm1) are the mass of absorbed solvent and dried rubber, respectively. Here α is the mass fraction of the rubber in the composites. ρs (toluene) = 0.8669 g/cm3,ρd (rubber) = 0.9743 g/cm3. Molar volume of toluene V1 = 106.3 cm3 /mol, Interaction parameters between NR and toluene x = 0.3795.

The thermo-oxidative resistance performance test consisted of two parts. The first part involved thermally-oxidizing all samples at 100℃ for 24 h in accordance with ASTM D573–2015. In the second part, NR-0 and NR-5 were subjected to heating at 100℃ and samples were taken out at 8, 16, 24, 32, and 40 h respectively, subsequently, the tensile properties, FTIR spectra and crosslinking density of samples at different thermal oxidation time were tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: the structure and fluorescence performance of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM spectra of CDs is shown in Fig. 1. It is evident that the size of the prepared CDs is approximately 10 nm, presenting a spherical shape with uniform particle dispersion and no obvious aggregation.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths of 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, and 420 nm. The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. It was observed that CDs have the ability to absorb short-wavelength excitation light and emit long-wavelength light, with the highest emission intensity occurring at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Moreover, as the excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelength initially rises and then falls. Additionally, there is a red shift in the emission wavelength with an increase in excitation wavelength, indicating a typical fluorescence excitation dependence (Yu et al., 2020). Similar findings have been reported in other studies where CDs were synthesized from banana peels (Atchudan et al., 2021; Edison et al., 2020).This fluorescence excitation dependence may be attributed to surface chemical bonds such as CO, C-OH, and C-O. These bonds result in different emission sites on the surface of CDs due to multiple electron transitions caused by various functional groups. Consequently, changing the excitation wavelength leads to different fluorescence emissions. CDs derived from biomass typically contain abundant surface functional groups; hence they often exhibit both fluorescence excitation dependence and good antioxidant activity (Xie and Schaich, 2014). The origins of the optical properties of CDs remain a subject of active debate and are not yet fully understood. Consequently, various mechanisms have been proposed to elucidate these properties, including quantum confinement effects, surface traps, the formation of aromatic structures, and exciton recombination (Zhang and Chen., 2014; Atchudan et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanical properties of NR/CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The representative tensile curves are depicted in Fig. 4, while Table 2 presents the characteristic mechanical properties of NR/CDs films with varying CDs loading. The tensile strength and tear strength of all samples showed improvement compared with those of NR-0, with increases of 3.9%, 27.0%, 16.2%, 17.4% and 15.1% for tensile strength, and increases of 22.6%, 31.5%, 42.3%, 40.4% and 38.9% for tear strength, respectively. T-tests conducted on the tensile strength and tear strength of NR/CDs films yielded statistics of t = 28.275 (p < 0.001) and t = 19.674 (p < 0.001), respectively, indicating a significant enhancement in both tensile and tear strengths with the incorporation of CDs.

Fig. 3 illustrates that at lower strains (<400%), the stress gradually rises with increasing strain, showing no significant difference between NR-0 and NR/CDs films due to the elevated stress caused by molecular chain entanglement (Pickering et al., 2016). When the loading of CDs reached 3 phr (NR-3) and 4 phr (NR-4), a peak in tensile strength and tear strength was observed, respectively. After reaching these peaks, both strengths slightly decreased with increased CDs loading. Tensile strength and tear strength serves as indicators of material's ability to resist fracture expansion, toughness, and durability; higher strength implies longer elastomer service life. Conversely, the addition of CDs did not damage the elongation at break of NR films; instead it exhibited an upward trend. It is noteworthy that the modulus of 300% and 500% does not monotonously increase with the increase of CDs content, indicating that the reinforcing mechanism of CDs differs from traditional fillers.

The crosslinking density of the NR film is shown in Fig. 4. The results indicated that as the amount of CDs increased, the crosslinking density initially rose, reaching its peak at NR-3, and then slightly decreased. The cross-linking density of NR-0 was 10.91×10-4 mol/cm3, whereas that of NR-3 was 12.49×10-4 mol/cm3, representing a 14.4% increase compared with NR-0. Generally, the presence of crosslinks enhances the strength and toughness of vulcanized rubber while reducing its elongation at break. The t-tests conducted on the crosslinking density data revealed a statistic of t = 36.225 (P<0.001), which exceeds the critical value of 2.37 at the 95% confidence level. This indicates a significant increase in crosslinking density with the addition of CDs.

To investigate the impact of CDs on the network structure of NR film, the tube model was employed to analyze the parameters of the network structure in accordance with equation (3) (Manfred and Joachim, 2000; Kutter and Terentjeva, 2001)
(3a)
σ
M
=
σ
λ −
λ
− 2
= Gc + Gef ( λ )
(3b)
f ( λ ) =
2 ( λ
β / 2
−
λ
− β
)
β (
λ
2
−
λ
− 1
)
f ( 1 ) = 1

Where σ and λ represent stress and elongation ratios, respectively. σM denotes the reduction of stress; Gc is the elastic modulus contributed by chemical crosslinking, while Ge represents the elastic modulus brought about by the constraint effect of entangled chains. The parameter β signifies the constraint release effect in the polymer network and is typically assigned a value of 1 (Zhan et al., 2020). The representative tensile curve data of NR film were substituted into equation (3) for calculation, and the f(λ)-σM curves are shown in Fig. 5a; Linear fitting was then performed according to the linear part of f(λ)-σM curves (f(λ) = 0.4–0.6)), and the results are shown in the Fig. 5b, where the intercept and slope of fitted linear equation represent Gc and Ge, respectively. The results of Gc and Ge are shown in Fig. 5c. The results indicate that the Ge value is significantly higher than the Gc value in this system, suggesting that the physical entangled network play a more significant role than chemical cross-linking. It is noteworthy that the trend of Gc+Ge with the amount of CDs aligns completely with the cross-linking density measured by swelling method. As the amount of CDs increases, Gc+Ge initially rises, reaching its peak at NR-3, and then slightly decreases (Punyarat and Pranee, 2024; Chen et al., 2024).

The schematic diagram depicting the influence of CDs on NR film crosslinking networks is presented in Scheme 2. Crosslinking agents, such as sulfur, are incorporated into the NR latex to improve toughness, tensile strength, and tear resistance. Covalent cross-linking bonds, such as monosulfur and polysulfur, are formed by rubber molecules during the vulcanization process. In the meanwhile, CDs may interact with NR chains mainly through physical adsorption. CTAB is a cationic surfactant, with its charge distribution predominantly localized on the nitrogen atoms of its quaternary ammonium salt, which carries a positive charge. In contrast, the elongated carbon chain exhibits hydrophobic characteristics. The surface of CDs typically bears a negative charge due to the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. This leads to electrostatic interactions between CTAB and CDs, facilitating the adsorption of CTAB molecules onto their surfaces. The interactions between the long-chain alkyl groups in CTAB and NR latex particles are primarily governed by hydrophobic interactions and physical adsorption. Due to their small size, CDs facilitate uniform dispersion throughout the NR matrix, thereby enhancing overall performance while alleviating potential inconsistencies associated with larger particle fillers. Findings from tube model calculations indicate that physical adsorption between CDs and rubber molecular chains predominates over chemical crosslinking. When external forces are applied—particularly at lower magnitudes—the molecular chains can slide along the surfaces of CDs without experiencing breakage. However, when the force reaches a sufficient magnitude, covalent bonds may break; nonetheless, physical adsorption between CDs and rubber can still be maintained within a certain distance. This results in increased tear and tensile strength while simultaneously allowing for greater elongation at break (Du,et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thermo-oxidative resistance performance of NR/CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: Elastomers, particularly diene rubber containing unsaturated double carbon bonds in the molecular chain, are susceptible to thermal and oxygen aging. This leads to a reduction in mechanical properties and elasticity, ultimately resulting in premature failure of elastomer products. Therefore, it is imperative that antioxidants be utilized to delay the thermal oxidation aging process of rubber composites and thereby prolong their service life (Ikeda et al., 1999).

The tensile and tear strength retention, Elongation at break retention, and aging coefficient were calculated according to the Eq. (4).
(4) Aging coefficient=((Ta×Ea）/(Tb×Eb))×100%

Where Ta and Ea represent the tensile strength and elongation at break of the after-aged samples,respectively; Tb and Eb are the tensile strength and elongation at break of the before-aged samples,respectively (Huangfu et al., 2021). The results are depicted in Fig. 6. It is evident that the aging coefficient increases with the amount of CDs. When the amount of CDs exceeds 3 phr, there is a noticeable acceleration in the increase rate. Specifically, when the amount of CDs increasing from 0 to 7 phr, the aging coefficient rose from 20.7% to 65.8%. The addition of CDs significantly enhanced the thermo-oxidative resistance properties of the NR film.

To gain a better understanding of the impact of CDs on the thermal oxidative degradation of NR, the NR-0 and NR-5 were selected as representatives to compare the effects of degradation time on crosslinking density and structure. An aging experiment was conducted to investigate the effect of thermal oxygen aging time on mechanical properties, structure, and crosslinking density within intervals of 0–40 hour with an interval of 8 hour. The results are presented in
Figs. 7 and 8.

It was observed that as aging time increased, both tensile strength and crosslinking density decreased. At an aging time of 40 hours, the tensile strength retention of NR-0 and NR-5 are 2.0% and 26.0%, respectively; The crosslinking density decreased significantly as the exposure time extended, and the rate of decline of NR film with CDs was noticeably slowed down.The crosslinking densities retention are 29.5% (NR-0) and 62.2% (NR-5) respectively; The tensile strength of NR-0 was reduced to 0.53 Mpa and almost completely failed, while the tensile strength of NR-5 was still maintained at 8.2 MPa. It can be seen that CDs has outstanding thermo-oxidative resistance properties for NR film.

The FTIR spectra of CDs and NR films were analyzed. It is well known that the orange peels contain cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and pectin, with cellulose being the predominant polysaccharide in plant biomass cell walls. In the FTIR spectra of CDs (Fig. 8a), the broad peak at 3400 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H. The characteristic peaks at 1655 cm-1 and 1020 cm-1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of -CO and C-O, respectively. The weak absorption peaks at 2921 cm-1 and 2851 cm-1 are related to -CH2. Hence, the FTIR spectra indicate that the surface of the CDs is rich in various functional groups, including -OH, CO, and C-O.

In the FTIR spectra of NR films (Fig. 8a), the absorption peaks at 2921 cm-1 and 2851 cm-1 correspond to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -CH2, respectively. The band at 1655 cm-1 represents a stretching vibration of -CC-. Additionally, wavebands at 1450 and 1347 cm-1 correspond to asymmetric vibration deformation peaks as well as symmetric vibration deformation peaks for C-H in -CH2-. Furthermore, an absorption peak around 835 cm-1 is due to a deformation vibration absorption peak for C-H in H-CC. In NR/CDs films, apart from the characteristic peaks of NR, the peak around 734 cm-1 was attributed to the C-S bond formed in the sulfur vulcanized network. Overall, the analysis indicates that both CDs and NR films exhibit distinct spectral features indicative of their chemical composition and functional group characteristics.

The process of thermal oxidation degradation is closely associated with the oxidation reaction between rubber and oxygen, as well as the type and content of sulfur bonds in rubber. When exposed to heat or shear conditions, the presence of oxygen leads to the attack on CC bond, which initiates the formation of allyl radicals in the polymeric backbone. This results in easy breakage of the main chain and sulfur chain of NR during thermal oxidation degradation. The FTIR spectra analysis (Figs. 8b and 8c) shows that there was no significant change in the main chain structure of rubber over different aging times. However, with prolonged exposure, weak absorption peaks at 1026 cm⁻¹ emerged, which were attributed to the S-O bond (Ren et al.,2024).Based on the results regarding mechanical properties and crosslinking density, it is evident that oxidation has occurred. While very low levels of O₂ reaction may not be clearly detectable by FTIR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM images of NR films
ce:section.ce:para: The scanning electron microscope (SEM) map in Fig. 9 illustrates the tensile fracture section of the NR films. It is evident that the section of the NR/CDs films is rougher compared with that of NR-0, indicating a higher susceptibility to fracturing under increased tensile force. This suggests a stronger interface force between filler and rubber molecules. Due to the excellent dispersion and compatibility of CDs, there are no aggregates present in the tensile fracture section of NR films.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of NR-C0 and NR/CDs is presented in Fig. 10. The relationship between weight ratio (%) and temperature indicates that, under heating in a nitrogen atmosphere, the weight loss process of all samples primarily occurs within the range of 300–450°C due to the breakage of the rubber molecular chain. The DTG curve is derived from differential processing of TGA data. The findings reveal that the maximum thermal decomposition temperature (Tp) for all samples is approximately 375℃, with a residual mass of about 2% after heating to 600℃, showing no significant difference. This suggests that the addition of CDs does not impact the thermal stability of the NR film.

In summary, this study has demonstrated that CDs derived from orange peel can be directly utilized to modify NR latex and act as an effective thermo-oxidative resistance without the need for expensive additives or a lengthy and cumbersome purification process. In order to achieve good dispersion of CDs in the matrix, NR/CDs composites were prepared by latex method in the presence of surfactant of CTAB. The mechanical properties and thermo-oxidative resistance properties of NR/CDs composites were studied by mechanical properties test, crosslinking density analysis, and FTIR spectroscopy. The results showed that CDs could significantly improve the mechanical properties of NR film without compromising its elasticity. Additionally, the addition of a small amount of CDs effectively inhibited the oxidation process of NR and significantly improved its anti-aging ability. These findings not only provide valuable information for the preparation of antioxidant enhanced NR composites, but also provide a simpler and more environmentally friendly method for the utilization of waste orange peels.

Zhang Fuquan: Data curation. Liao Lusheng: Writing – review & editing. Jing Chen: Writing – original draft. Du Lijuan: Investigation. Shang Lingyu: Investigation. Liu Jiahui: Investigation. Lai Yinyi: Investigation. Ma Yukun: Investigation. Yang Zinuo: Investigation. Peng WenFeng: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Jing Chen, Lusheng Liao reports financial support was provided by Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System. Lusheng Liao reports financial support was provided by the Foundation of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Natural Rubber Processing. Fuquan Zhang reports financial support was provided by Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work were financially supported by Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System, Grant/Award Number: CARS-33-JG2; Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund Grant/Award Number 1630122022006; College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training (X202410579003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, S., Song, Y., Zhao, X.,Shao，J., Zhang, J.，Bai, Y., 2015. The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective.Nano Research 8(002),355-381. http://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-014-0644-3.
